---
title: "ModelLens: A General-Purpose Mechanistic Interpretability Toolkit for PyTorch Neural Networks"
author:
  - name: "Team Member 2"
    affiliations:
      - name: "Georgetown University"  # UPDATE with your actual university
  - name: "Team Member 2"
    affiliations:
      - name: "Georgetown University"
  - name: "Team Member 3"
    affiliations:
      - name: "Georgetown University"
  - name: "Team Member 4"
    affiliations:
      - name: "Georgetown University"
  - name: "Team Member 5"
    affiliations:
      - name: "Georgetown University"
date: today
abstract: |
  Understanding the internal mechanisms of neural networks is critical for
  ensuring safety, enabling debugging, and advancing scientific insight into
  learned representations. We present ModelLens, an open-source Python toolkit
  that brings mechanistic interpretability techniques to arbitrary PyTorch
  models. Unlike existing tools such as TransformerLens, which is restricted
  to transformer architectures, or Captum, which focuses on attribution rather
  than mechanistic analysis, ModelLens provides architecture-agnostic support
  for logit lens projection, activation patching, attention visualization,
  residual stream analysis, and embedding inspection through a unified
  forward-hook-based infrastructure. We validate the toolkit by training a
  small transformer on three-digit addition and using ModelLens to
  reverse-engineer the learned algorithm, recovering interpretable structure
  consistent with prior findings in the mechanistic interpretability
  literature. We further demonstrate generalizability by applying ModelLens
  to pretrained HuggingFace models. A Gradio-based web interface enables
  interactive exploration without requiring users to write code. The toolkit
  and all experimental artifacts are publicly available.
keywords:
  - mechanistic interpretability
  - neural network analysis
  - transformer circuits
  - activation patching
  - logit lens
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    number-sections: true
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.5          # ~ double-spaced for draft review
    link-citations: true
    colorlinks: true
    fig-pos: "htbp"
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true           # lets readers expand/collapse code blocks
bibliography: references.bib
csl: ieee.csl                # CHANGE this to match your target venue style
                              # e.g., apa.csl, acm-sig-proceedings.csl
                              # download CSL files from https://www.zotero.org/styles
---

<!-- ============================================================
     HOW TO USE THIS TEMPLATE
     ============================================================
     1. Install Quarto: https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
     2. Place this file and references.bib in the same folder
     3. Download a CSL style file if needed (or remove the csl: line
        to use the default Chicago style)
     4. Render:  quarto render report.qmd
     5. Inline citations:  [@nanda2023progress] or [@meng2022locating]
        Multiple:  [@nanda2023progress; @elhage2021mathematical]
        Parenthetical with page: [@vaswani2017attention, p. 3]
     ============================================================ -->


# Introduction

<!-- ~1.5 pages. Frame the problem, state contributions. -->

Modern neural networks achieve strong performance across a wide range of
tasks, yet their internal decision-making processes remain largely opaque.
This opacity poses challenges for debugging unexpected model behavior,
auditing models for regulatory compliance, conducting AI safety research,
and advancing scientific understanding of learned representations
[@olah2020zoom].

The field of *mechanistic interpretability* seeks to address this gap by
treating trained networks as objects of scientific study—reverse-engineering
the algorithms they learn by inspecting, visualizing, and intervening on
internal computations [@elhage2021mathematical]. Several tools exist to
support this work, but each has significant limitations. TransformerLens
[@nanda2022transformerlens] provides rich mechanistic analysis capabilities
but is restricted to transformer architectures. Captum [@kokhlikyan2020captum]
supports arbitrary PyTorch models but focuses on gradient-based attribution
methods rather than the causal and representational analyses central to
mechanistic interpretability.

In this paper, we present **ModelLens**, an open-source Python toolkit that
bridges this gap. ModelLens provides architecture-agnostic mechanistic
interpretability through a unified forward-hook-based infrastructure,
supporting logit lens projection, activation patching, attention
visualization, residual stream analysis, and embedding inspection for any
PyTorch model.

Our contributions are as follows:

1. **A general-purpose interpretability toolkit** that applies mechanistic
   analysis techniques to arbitrary `nn.Module` and HuggingFace
   `PreTrainedModel` architectures through a backend-adapter design pattern.
2. **A validation study** in which we train a small transformer on three-digit
   addition, achieve approximately 93% accuracy, and use ModelLens to
   reverse-engineer the learned algorithm.
3. **A Gradio-based web interface** that makes mechanistic interpretability
   accessible to users without programming experience.

<!-- TODO: Add a sentence about the paper's structure if your professor
     expects a "roadmap" paragraph. -->


# Related Work {#sec-related-work}

<!-- ~2 pages. Group by theme, not chronologically. Cite heavily. -->

## Mechanistic Interpretability

The circuits-based approach to interpretability, pioneered by @olah2020zoom,
treats neural network components as implementing identifiable algorithms.
@elhage2021mathematical formalized this perspective for transformers,
introducing the *residual stream* view in which attention heads and MLP layers
read from and write to a shared representational space. @olsson2022context
extended this framework by identifying *induction heads*—a specific circuit
motif responsible for in-context learning. @elhage2022toy further explored how
networks represent multiple features in superposition within this framework.

## Logit Lens and Intermediate Predictions

The logit lens technique, introduced by @nostalgebraist2020logitlens, projects
intermediate layer activations through a model's final unembedding matrix to
observe how predictions evolve across layers. @belrose2023eliciting refined
this approach with the *tuned lens*, which learns an affine transformation at
each layer to produce better-calibrated intermediate predictions.

## Activation Patching and Causal Analysis

Activation patching—systematically replacing activations from one forward pass
with those from another to measure causal effects—was formalized by
@meng2022locating in their work on locating factual associations in GPT.
Related causal mediation approaches were applied by @vig2020investigating to
study gender bias. @conmy2023automated developed automated circuit discovery
(ACDC), which scales activation patching to identify minimal circuits
responsible for specific behaviors.

## Attention Analysis

@clark2019what provided an early systematic analysis of attention patterns in
BERT. The relationship between attention weights and model behavior has been
debated: @jain2019attention argued that attention does not constitute
explanation, while @wiegreffe2019attention demonstrated conditions under which
attention patterns do carry explanatory value. This debate motivates the
inclusion of *both* attention visualization and causal methods (activation
patching) in ModelLens.

## Modular Arithmetic and Algorithmic Interpretability

Small transformers trained on algorithmic tasks provide valuable test cases
for interpretability because the learned algorithms can be independently
verified. @nanda2023progress showed that transformers trained on modular
addition learn trigonometric representations, and used this finding to develop
*progress measures* that track the formation of generalizing circuits during
training. @power2022grokking first described the *grokking* phenomenon—delayed
generalization long after memorization—on such tasks. @nogueira2021investigating
studied the limitations of transformers on simple arithmetic, providing context
for our choice of three-digit addition as a demonstration task.

## Existing Tools

**TransformerLens** [@nanda2022transformerlens] is the most widely used
mechanistic interpretability library. It provides activation caching, hook
points, and direct logit attribution but requires models to conform to its
internal `HookedTransformer` architecture.

**Captum** [@kokhlikyan2020captum] is a general-purpose interpretability
library for PyTorch that supports integrated gradients, SHAP, and other
attribution methods. However, it does not provide mechanistic techniques
such as logit lens or activation patching.

ModelLens occupies a distinct position: it combines the mechanistic analysis
depth of TransformerLens with the architectural flexibility of Captum.


# System Design and Architecture {#sec-system-design}

<!-- ~3 pages. This is YOUR technical contribution. Be precise. -->

## Design Goals

ModelLens was designed around four principles:

1. **Architecture agnosticism.** The toolkit should work with any PyTorch
   `nn.Module` without requiring architectural modifications.
2. **Minimal user configuration.** Users should be able to wrap a model and
   begin analysis in two lines of code.
3. **Extensibility.** New analysis techniques should be addable without
   modifying the core infrastructure.
4. **Accessibility.** A web-based interface should expose all analyses to
   non-programmers.

## Core Infrastructure: Hook-Based Activation Caching

The foundation of ModelLens is PyTorch's forward hook system
[@paszke2019pytorch]. When `model.register_forward_hook(fn)` is called on a
module, the provided function receives the module's input and output tensors
during every forward pass. ModelLens registers hooks at every layer of
interest, caching intermediate activations in a dictionary keyed by layer
name.

<!-- TODO: Insert a code listing or pseudocode block here showing the
     hook registration pattern with the closure for correct scoping.
     Example:

```python
cache = {}
def make_hook(name):
    def hook_fn(module, input, output):
        cache[name] = output.detach()
    return hook_fn
for name, module in model.named_modules():
    module.register_forward_hook(make_hook(name))
```
-->

The closure-based design ensures correct scoping: without the inner function
factory, all hooks would overwrite the same dictionary key due to Python's
late-binding behavior in loops.

## Backend Adapters

ModelLens supports two model backends through an adapter pattern:

- **PyTorch adapter** for vanilla `nn.Module` models. It uses
  `named_modules()` to discover layers and infers layer types from module
  class names.
- **HuggingFace adapter** for `PreTrainedModel` architectures. It leverages
  the standardized naming conventions in HuggingFace models (e.g.,
  `model.transformer.h[i]`) to identify attention heads, MLP blocks, and
  layer norms.

<!-- TODO: Consider adding an architecture diagram (Figure 1) showing the
     relationship between ModelLens, adapters, analysis modules, and the
     Gradio UI. -->

## Analysis Modules

### Logit Lens

The logit lens module projects the hidden state $h_l$ at layer $l$ through
the model's final layer normalization and unembedding matrix $W_U$ to produce
a distribution over the vocabulary at each intermediate layer:

$$p_l = \text{softmax}(W_U \cdot \text{LayerNorm}(h_l))$$

This reveals how the model's "beliefs" about the output token evolve as
information flows through the network.

### Activation Patching

Given a *clean* input $x_c$ and a *corrupted* input $x_p$, activation
patching measures the causal importance of a component $C$ at layer $l$ by:

1. Running the model on $x_c$ to cache all activations.
2. Running the model on $x_p$, but replacing the activation at component $C$,
   layer $l$ with the cached clean activation.
3. Measuring how much the output shifts back toward the clean output.

Formally, the patching effect for component $C$ at layer $l$ is:

$$\Delta_{C,l} = \mathcal{L}(x_p) - \mathcal{L}(x_p \mid h_{C,l} \leftarrow h_{C,l}^{\text{clean}})$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is the loss function. Large values indicate that
component $C$ at layer $l$ is causally important for the behavior of interest.

### Attention Visualization

<!-- TODO: Describe your attention extraction and visualization approach.
     Address the attention-as-explanation debate (Jain vs Wiegreffe). -->

### Residual Stream Analysis

<!-- TODO: Describe how you decompose the residual stream into per-layer
     contributions and measure each layer's impact on the final output. -->

### Embedding Inspection

<!-- TODO: Describe your tools for inspecting input/output embedding spaces,
     including any dimensionality reduction for visualization. -->

## Gradio Interface

<!-- TODO: Describe the web interface design, what controls are exposed
     (model selection, input data, layer selection, analysis type),
     and include a screenshot (Figure 2). -->


# Demonstration: Arithmetic Transformer {#sec-demonstration}

<!-- ~2 pages. Describe the model and training. -->

## Task Definition

We train a small transformer to perform three-digit addition (e.g.,
$123 + 456 = 579$). Inputs are tokenized as sequences of individual digits
with operator and equals-sign delimiters. This task was chosen because:

- The correct algorithm (carry-propagation) is fully known, enabling
  verification of interpretability findings.
- The model is small enough to analyze exhaustively.
- Prior work on arithmetic in transformers [@nogueira2021investigating]
  provides baseline expectations.

## Model Architecture

<!-- TODO: Fill in your actual hyperparameters from ArithmeticTransformer -->

The `ArithmeticTransformer` consists of:

| Hyperparameter       | Value          |
|-----------------------|---------------|
| Embedding dimension   | <!-- TODO --> |
| Number of layers      | <!-- TODO --> |
| Attention heads       | <!-- TODO --> |
| MLP hidden dimension  | <!-- TODO --> |
| Vocabulary size       | <!-- TODO --> |
| Max sequence length   | <!-- TODO --> |
| Total parameters      | ~808,704      |

The model uses learned positional embeddings, pre-norm layer normalization,
GELU activations in the MLP blocks, and a causal attention mask to enforce
autoregressive generation of the output digits.

## Training

<!-- TODO: Fill in training details -->

The model was trained using:

- **Optimizer:** AdamW with learning rate <!-- TODO -->
- **Loss function:** Cross-entropy with masking (loss computed only on output
  digit positions)
- **Training data:** Randomly generated addition problems
- **Training duration:** <!-- TODO epochs/steps -->
- **Final accuracy:** Approximately 93% on held-out test problems

<!-- TODO: Include a training loss curve (Figure 3). -->


# Interpretability Results {#sec-results}

<!-- ~3-4 pages. This is the core scientific contribution. Show figures. -->

## Logit Lens Analysis

<!-- TODO: Present logit lens results as a heatmap (Figure 4) showing how
     the model's prediction for each output digit evolves across layers.
     Key questions to address:
     - At which layer does the model "decide" on the correct output digit?
     - Does carry resolution happen at a specific layer?
     - Do different digit positions resolve at different layers? -->

## Attention Patterns

<!-- TODO: Present attention maps (Figure 5) for representative inputs.
     Key questions:
     - Do attention heads learn to attend to specific operand positions?
     - Is there a head that specializes in carry detection?
     - How do patterns differ for problems with vs. without carries? -->

## Activation Patching

<!-- TODO: Present a patching heatmap (Figure 6) showing which
     layers/components are causally important.
     Key questions:
     - Which layers are critical for carry propagation?
     - Are there identifiable "circuits" for addition vs. carry? -->

## Residual Stream Decomposition

<!-- TODO: Show per-layer contributions to the final logits.
     Does each layer add a specific piece of the computation? -->

## Comparison with Prior Work

<!-- TODO: Compare your findings to Nanda et al.'s results on modular
     arithmetic. Do you see analogous structure (e.g., trigonometric
     representations, or in this case, digit-position-specific circuits)?
     Be honest about what aligns and what differs. -->


# Generalizability {#sec-generalizability}

<!-- ~1-1.5 pages. Show ModelLens works beyond the demo model. -->

## Application to Pretrained Models

<!-- TODO: Show ModelLens applied to GPT-2 or another HuggingFace model.
     Present at least one analysis (e.g., logit lens on a text completion
     task) and demonstrate that the same API works across backends. -->

## Comparison with TransformerLens

<!-- TODO: For the same analysis on a HuggingFace model, compare the
     user experience (lines of code, setup required, flexibility) between
     ModelLens and TransformerLens. Be fair — note where TransformerLens
     is more polished or feature-rich. -->

## Limitations Discovered During Generalization

<!-- TODO: Be honest about edge cases, models that didn't work well,
     or analyses that required backend-specific adjustments. -->


# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

<!-- ~1-1.5 pages. -->

## Summary of Findings

<!-- TODO: Synthesize: what did the arithmetic transformer teach us about
     both the model AND the toolkit? -->

## Limitations

<!-- TODO: Be specific. E.g.:
     - Forward hooks only; no backward hooks for gradient-based analysis
     - No automated circuit discovery (ACDC-style)
     - Hook overhead for very large models
     - Logit lens assumes a meaningful unembedding matrix -->

## Ethical Considerations

Mechanistic interpretability tools are inherently dual-use. While they
enable safety auditing, bias detection, and regulatory compliance, they
could also be used to identify and exploit model vulnerabilities. We
believe the benefits of transparency outweigh the risks, but acknowledge
this tension.

## Future Work

<!-- TODO: E.g.:
     - Backward hooks for gradient-based mechanistic analysis
     - Automated circuit discovery (ACDC integration)
     - Support for convolutional and recurrent architectures
     - Scaling analysis to larger models (memory optimization)
     - Integration with training-time interpretability (progress measures) -->


# Conclusion

<!-- ~0.5 page. Restate contributions concisely. No new information. -->

We presented ModelLens, a general-purpose mechanistic interpretability
toolkit for PyTorch neural networks. By leveraging forward hooks and a
backend-adapter design pattern, ModelLens brings logit lens, activation
patching, attention visualization, and residual stream analysis to
arbitrary model architectures. We validated the toolkit by
reverse-engineering a small transformer trained on three-digit addition,
recovering interpretable structure consistent with prior findings in the
mechanistic interpretability literature. The toolkit, including a
Gradio-based web interface for interactive exploration, is publicly
available.

<!-- TODO: Add your GitHub repo URL here once ready. -->


# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::


<!-- ============================================================
     APPENDIX — uncomment and fill in as needed
     ============================================================ -->

<!--
# Appendix {.unnumbered}

## A. Full Model Hyperparameters and Training Curves {.unnumbered}

## B. Additional Visualizations {.unnumbered}

## C. ModelLens API Usage Examples {.unnumbered}

## D. Gradio Interface Screenshots {.unnumbered}
-->
